details.text: Derivative: which means “when” is used in Arabic to remind people of something that is going to happen that they have forgotten or are being heedless of. إِذَا (When) is used to refer to the future. It also implies reminding about something that is bound to happen i.e., when and not if.

Derivative: زُلْزِلَتِ is the passive form from زَلْزَلَ. It is a verb which has four letters in its root رُبَاعِي (Rubaa’iy). It has been argued that even these verbs have an origin in verbs which have three letters in the root ثُلَاثِي (Thulaathiy). زَلَّ (Zalla) means to slip. When you have a repetition in the syllables like in زَلْزَلَ (Zalzala) it indicates a repetition in meaning. As such زَلْزَلَ can the repeated act of slipping and tripping over. زَلْزَلَ has the meaning of to shake and convulse in Arabic and زَلْزَلَة refers to an earthquake. The connection between the two words is that when the earth or ground shakes and convulses in an earthquake people find it hard to keep their feet upon the ground whilst they slip and trip. The use of the passive form in Arabic like زُلْزِلَتِ (will be shaken) implies greater ease in respect to the undertaking of the action that the active voice. The رُبَاعِي (Rubaa’iy) verb form has a quality of referring to an action that repeats itself, in terms of starting and stopping in a repeated fashion.

إِذَا زُلْزِلَتِ الْأَرْضُ زِلْزَالَهَا

- زِلْزَالَ (shaking or convulsion) can be understood to be المَفْهُوم المُطْلَق (Al-Mafhoom Al-Mutlaq) of the verb زُلْزِلَتِ (to be shaken). Al-Mafhoom Al-Mutlaq is a repetition of the root verb by using the verb and then the Masdar. Its purpose is to add emphasis and power to the action. As such إِذَا زُلْزِلَتِ الْأَرْضُ زِلْزَالَهَا can be translated as: “When the earth is shaken with its violent or severe convulsion”. The earthquake is therefore transformed into an incredible earthquake.
- However, because the المَفْهُوم المُطْلَق (Al-Mafhoom Al-Mutlaq) is usually in the form of the Masdar in the accusative form which would be زِلْزَالًا (Zilzaalan) here, then the addition of the ضَمِير (pronoun) to it of هَا in زِلْزَالَهَا can lead to an alternative or additional meaning. The verse would literally mean: “When the earth is shaken by its shaking or earthquake” and would imply that it is such an earthquake that no other can be occurred to it i.e., it is referring to the real and true shaking of the earth and what has been experienced prior to it cannot even be considered comparable to it. It refers to an unimaginable unique occurrence.
- Another understanding that can be derived is that زِلْزَالَهَا (Its earthquake) can indicate that it is the earth’s earthquake and it is fulfilling its promise, destiny or final purpose to shake on that day, the Day of Judgement.
- The pronoun attached in زِلْزَالَهَا (Its earthquake) can also imply that the earth will be shaken to exhaustion and to its greatest and utmost limits and capacity to shake and convulse.
- the pronoun attached in زِلْزَالَهَا (its earthquake) also implies that the whole earth will be shaken as the shaking زِلْزَالَهَا (its earthquake) is attached to the earth (الْأَرْضُ) as a whole.
Derivative: أَخْرَجَتِ from أَخْرَجَ – إِخْراج which means to take something out of something. It can mean to extract and to expel.

Derivative: أَثْقَالَ (Athqaal) is the plural of ثِقْلٌ (Thiql) and refers to heavy items, travel baggage, household goods and commodities. It refers to something with weight and is used to refer to a burden or something with gravity. The scholars of Tafsir have said that the أَثْقَالَ (Athqaal - Burdens) which will be ejected from the earth includes what was buried under it in terms of the dead and treasures. It has also been said that the burdens include the recorded actions of people as the earth is a witness to them and their sinful acts have continuously been piling up within it. It is as if the earth will finally be able to get relief from its accumulated pain.

Derivative: الْإِنسَانُ (Al-Insaan) means “the human being”. It is said that it comes from the verb نَسِيَ which means to forget and is applicable to the human being because he is forgetful by nature. Its usage fits here in this context because what follows is the speech of the Insaan in shock and surprise of what is taking place in terms of the final violent earthquake and Day of Judgement, a matter that he forgot and was heedless about. The use of the singular here of الْإِنسَانُ (Al-Insaan) also indicates that each person will be forced to face this reality by themselves as isolated individuals.

- مَا followed by ل is an Arabic expression that means: “What is wrong with something?” or “What is the matter with something?”. مَا لَكَ therefore means: “What is the matter or wrong with you?” and مَا لَهَا here means “What is wrong with it?” i.e., what is wrong or the matter with the earth? Why is it behaving in this way?

Derivative: أَخْبَارَ is the plural of خَبَر. The words خَبَر (Khabar) and نَبَأُ (Naba’) are both used to mean news. However, نَبَأُ (Naba’) can refer to the news or information that you could never have possibly known about unless someone provided it to you. نَبِي (Nabiy - A Prophet) is derived from نَبَأَ (Naba’a) because the Prophet informs the people of that which they could not have known or had access to. خَبَر (Khabar) is something that is possible to be figured out or deciphered. نَبَأُ (Naba’) can refer to information about the past, present and future, whilst خَبَر (Khabar) is not used for the future. نَبَأُ (Naba’) can relate to that which one does not expect whilst خَبَر (Khabar) is used in relation to what can be expected.
Derivative: يَوْمَئِذٍ is more emphatic ظَرْف (Zharf) than إِذًا and it means “on that day”.

Derivative: تُحَدِّثُ from حَدَّثَ which means to make someone aware of something or to tell or talk about something. حَدَثَ – حُدُوث means to relates to the existence of something new that was not previously there. حَدَّثَ can refer to telling someone something that they did not previous know, to tell them something that they had forgotten and it is as if they are hearing it for the first time, and when the speaker uses language assuming that those being addressed have never heard about what he is speaking about before. The use of the verb تُحَدِّثُ therefore encompasses the meanings that every last detail will be spoken and informed about, reminding about that which had been forgotten whilst being received as if it had been heard for the first time.

يَوْمَئِذٍ تُحَدِّثُ أَخْبَارَهَا

- “On that day (يَوْمَئِذٍ) it will tell or inform about (تُحَدِّثُ) its news (أَخْبَارَهَا)” – This means that the earth on that day will inform and speak about everything that has been done by the people and will be a witness against them.

Derivative: أَوْحَىٰ – يُوْحِي – إِيحاء linguistically means to hint or gesture something to someone secretly. It means to inspire in a way that is hidden but the meaning is understood by the one who gives it and the one receiving it. The scholars of Tafsir have stated that the meaning of أَوْحَى here is the “He commanded” i.e., which a special divine command.

- أَوْحَى is usually used with the preposition إِلَى (Ilaa - to) however here the preposition لِ (for) is attached to it. In Arabic when a different preposition to the normal one is used it can imply more meaning in the case where a verb which is normally attached to the unusually used pronoun (which is لِ here) has been omitted in text but can be implicitly understood or understood with other connotations. When this happens, the phrase can include meanings of both verbs at the same time. It has been said that the missing verb here could be أَذِنَ (to be permitted) like what was stated in Surah Al-Inshiqaaq verse 3-5).

- The verses have been talking about the earth and have been using the pronoun of هَا (her or its) making everything relate to the earth (Al-Ard - الْأَرْضُ). However, instead of رَبَّهَا (Its Rabb – Lord and Master) it states رَبَّكَ “Your Rabb (Lord and Master)” referring to the Messenger of Allah ﷺ which can indicate to his special status and particularly on the Day of Judgement. Everything in these verses is second person which implies distance but when رَبّ (Rabb – Lord or Master) is mentioned it is attached to the Messenger ﷺ which implies closeness.

Derivative: أَشْتَاتًا is the plural of شَتِيت or شَتٌّ and refers to something that is broken up, separated and dispersed. أَشْتَاتًا means to be divided and dispersed into various different parts, groups or categories.

Derivative: أَعْمَالَ is the plural of عَمَلٌ and means actions or deeds. عَمَلٌ (‘Amal) implies those actions that you undertake consciously with intent whilst فِعْل (Fi’l) includes the subconscious acts. For example, hearing and seeing are both a فِعْل (Fi’l) whilst listening and looking are both an عَمَلٌ (‘Amal).

Derivative: يَصْدُرُ if from صَدْر and means to return from drinking i.e., from a water source he goes to drink from. It is said that people صَدَرَ from the water source when they leave and depart from it to return to where they had come from. It has been said that the people will return from the place of account to be shown the consequences of their deeds. It has also been said that this refers to being taken to the place of account to be shown their deeds.

Derivative: خَيْرًا (Good) is in the مَنْصوب (Mansoob – Accusative case) form. One grammatical explanation is that this is because it follows from مِثْقَالَ (Mithqaal) which is in the مَنْصوب (Mansoob – Accusative case) form. Another explanation is that it is in the مَنْصوب (Mansoob – Accusative case) form because it is a تَمْيِيز (a distinguisher) which can be translated as “in terms of” i.e., in terms of خَيْر (good).

Derivative: يَرَ from رَأَى – يَرَى – رُؤْيَة means to see literally and figuratively i.e., to actually see something physically and also to see something like an idea in terms of understanding it e.g., I see what you are saying or how do you see such and such a matter? The use of يَرَى “He will see” can therefore combine both meanings or seeing and also understanding what he sees.

Derivative: ذَرَّةٍ is used to refer to the smallest thing imaginable. The Arabs would call an egg laid by an ant or the smallest ant a ذَرَّةٍ (Dharrah) because it was the smallest thing they could imagine. It has been used to describe the little fibres of dust that can be seen floating in sunlight. Today we would call the most miniscule things we could imagine a speck or an atom.

Derivative: مِثْقَالَ (Mithqaal) is a tool used to weigh. It refers to a weight in terms of a measure and not in terms of actually being heavy. It has been said to be used to refer to something that is little that can be weighed.

- Weight is not usually considered with the most miniscule things and مِثْقَالَ ذَرَّةٍ is therefore an expression to indicate to the most miniscule of acts here, so small that they are virtually weightless.
Derivative: شَرًّا comes from شَرَارَة which is a spark of fire and it refers to that which is understood to be evil.
